## Stand-alone widget tree with multiple render trees to enable multi-view rendering

Part of https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/121573.

This change enables Flutter to generate multiple Scenes to be rendered into separate FlutterViews from a single widget tree. Each Scene is described by a separate render tree, which are all associated with the single widget tree.

This PR implements the framework-side mechanisms to describe the content to be rendered into multiple views. Separate engine-side changes are necessary to provide these views to the framework and to draw the framework-generated Scene into them.

## Summary of changes

The details of this change are described in [flutter.dev/go/multiple-views](https://flutter.dev/go/multiple-views). Below is a high-level summary organized by layers.

### Rendering layer changes

* The `RendererBinding` no longer owns a single `renderView`. In fact, it doesn't OWN any `RenderView`s at all anymore. Instead, it offers an API (`addRenderView`/`removeRenderView`) to add and remove `RenderView`s that then will be MANAGED by the binding. The `RenderView` itself is now owned by a higher-level abstraction (e.g. the `RawView` Element of the widgets layer, see below), who is also in charge of adding it to the binding. When added, the binding will interact with the `RenderView` to produce a frame (e.g. by calling `compositeFrame` on it) and to perform hit tests for incoming pointer events. Multiple `RenderView`s can be added to the binding (typically one per `FlutterView`) to produce multiple Scenes.
* Instead of owning a single `pipelineOwner`, the `RendererBinding` now owns the root of the `PipelineOwner` tree (exposed as `rootPipelineOwner` on the binding). Each `PipelineOwner` in that tree (except for the root) typically manages its own render tree typically rooted in one of the `RenderView`s mentioned in the previous bullet. During frame production, the binding will instruct each `PipelineOwner` of that tree to flush layout, paint, semantics etc. A higher-level abstraction (e.g. the widgets layer, see below) is in charge of adding `PipelineOwner`s to this tree.
* Backwards compatibility: The old `renderView` and `pipelineOwner` properties of the `RendererBinding` are retained, but marked as deprecated. Care has been taken to keep their original behavior for the deprecation period, i.e. if you just call `runApp`, the render tree bootstrapped by this call is rooted in the deprecated `RendererBinding.renderView` and managed by the deprecated `RendererBinding.pipelineOwner`.

### Widgets layer changes

* The `WidgetsBinding` no longer attaches the widget tree to an existing render tree. Instead, it bootstraps a stand-alone widget tree that is not backed by a render tree. For this, `RenderObjectToWidgetAdapter` has been replaced by `RootWidget`.
* Multiple render trees can be bootstrapped and attached to the widget tree with the help of the `View` widget, which internally is backed by a `RawView` widget. Configured with a `FlutterView` to render into, the `RawView` creates a new `PipelineOwner` and a new `RenderView` for the new render tree. It adds the new `RenderView` to the `RendererBinding` and its `PipelineOwner` to the pipeline owner tree.
* The `View` widget can only appear in certain well-defined locations in the widget tree since it bootstraps a new render tree and does not insert a `RenderObject` into an ancestor. However, almost all Elements expect that their children insert `RenderObject`s, otherwise they will not function properly. To produce a good error message when the `View` widget is used in an illegal location, the `debugMustInsertRenderObjectIntoSlot` method has been added to Element, where a child can ask whether a given slot must insert a RenderObject into its ancestor or not. In practice, the `View` widget can be used as a child of the `RootWidget`, inside the `view` slot of the `ViewAnchor` (see below) and inside a `ViewCollection` (see below). In those locations, the `View` widget may be wrapped in other non-RenderObjectWidgets (e.g. InheritedWidgets).
* The new `ViewAnchor` can be used to create a side-view inside a parent `View`. The `child` of the `ViewAnchor` widget renders into the parent `View` as usual, but the `view` slot can take on another `View` widget, which has access to all inherited widgets above the `ViewAnchor`. Metaphorically speaking, the view is anchored to the location of the `ViewAnchor` in the widget tree.
* The new `ViewCollection` widget allows for multiple sibling views as it takes a list of `View`s as children. It can be used in all the places that accept a `View` widget.

## Google3

As of July 5, 2023 this change passed a TAP global presubmit (TGP) in google3: tap/OCL:544707016:BASE:545809771:1688597935864:e43dd651

## Note to reviewers

This change is big (sorry). I suggest focusing the initial review on the changes inside of `packages/flutter` first. The majority of the changes describe above are implemented in (listed in suggested review order):

* `rendering/binding.dart`
* `widgets/binding.dart`
* `widgets/view.dart`
* `widgets/framework.dart`

All other changes included in the PR are basically the fallout of what's implemented in those files. Also note that a lot of the lines added in this PR are documentation and tests.

I am also very happy to walk reviewers through the code in person or via video call, if that is helpful.

I appreciate any feedback.

## Feedback to address before submitting ("TODO")

- [x] PipelineOwner tree should implement `DiagnosticableTree`
- [x] ViewHooksScope should be private
- [x] Disallow setting root node on default root pipeline owner

Inspired from some of the comments here I removed some of the concepts and abstractions from this change: The `ViewHooks` and the `RenderViewManager` are gone. I believe, they just made things harder to understand (by introducing more indirection) without benefit.

With Monday's fresh set of eyes I also realized that there is little point in `RawView` being public. It really is just an implementation detail of `View` and not useful for anything on its own. So, I ended up making it private.
Added a new doc section "relationship to render trees" to `WidgetsBinding` explaining the rendering and non-rendering zones of the element tree and addressed the other doc comments.
All outstanding feedback should be addressed now.
The new comments are awesome, thanks for adding them! 🎉

## ☂️ Move the material and cupertino packages outside of Flutter

This issue tracks the work to formally decouple the material and cupertino libraries from the core Flutter framework and establish them as standalone, first-party packages on pub.dev. 

➡️  **📄  [Read more about our plan.](https://docs.google.com/document/d/189AbzVGpxhQczTcdfJd13o_EL36t-M5jOEt1hgBIh7w/edit?usp=sharing)** ⬅️ 

This strategic transition will make Flutter's core lighter and more flexible, accelerate design library updates, and give developers more control over their app's dependencies. 

The work will be managed through a deliberate, three-phased approach to ensure a stable and predictable migration for the entire ecosystem. 

The sub-issues listed below track the specific tasks for each phase of this project.

### 📣 To provide feedback:
  - Generally? **Here.**
  - On critical refactoring of code out of material/cupertino before decoupling:
    - https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/53059
  - On net-new additions of core components:
    - https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/97496
  - On the new packages: 
    - https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/172932
  - On future deprecation and removal: 
    - https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/172942

---

<details> <summary> OP </summary>

## Use case

- Long update cycle
- To get the new version - you need to update the version of Flutter
- Random imports
- Components for other platforms are separate from Flutter ([fluent](https://pub.dev/packages/fluent), [macos_ui](https://pub.dev/packages/macos_ui), [yaru](https://pub.dev/packages/yaru), ...)

## Proposal

Move `material` and `cupertino` packages from Flutter

It can be done along with this issue (https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/91605)

## Update 04/18/25

[Link to proposal](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/101479#issuecomment-2661443881) by @MaherSafadii with examples.

</details>

Hi @nikitadol, Thanks for filing the issue. If I understand it right moving material and Cupertino outside of flutter means, the flutter SDK wouldn't have any of the default material and Cupertino widgets, unless they are explicitly imported from pub. 
I believe doing so would lead to inconsistency and duplication of code across both packages. 

> Components for other platforms are separate from Flutter ([fluent](https://pub.dev/packages/fluent), [macos_ui](https://pub.dev/packages/macos_ui), [yaru](https://pub.dev/packages/yaru), ...)

the packages that you linked are third-party packages so they are not part of the framework.

Labeling this issue for further thoughts from the team.

cc: @rami-a 

Edit: The inconsistency and duplication I was referring to is the code which is being reused across both material and cupertino

> I believe doing so would lead to inconsistency and duplication of code across both packages.

The `material` package depends on `cupertino` - I do not quite understand what the problem will be

I think about this case and I found it is a good idea but in the long-term, it will lead to a fragmented community
Like a company uses AmazonButton and a different company using AppleButton so changing a company to the next company that uses different UI names and parameters will be a bad experience

> I think about this case and I found it is a good idea but in the long-term, it will lead to a fragmented community Like a company uses AmazonButton and a different company using AppleButton so changing a company to the next company that uses different UI names and parameters will be a bad experience

Except that scenario is very unlikely to happen, why would a company change its flutter app to use a totally different design system? Also right now this is not possible either, as cupertino and material have different widgets as well.

There are community efforts to bring together multiple design system in a platform agnostic way i think they are called `platform-widgets` or something like that.

> Thanks for filing the issue. If I understand it right moving material and Cupertino outside of flutter means, the flutter SDK wouldn't have any of the default material and Cupertino widgets, unless they are explicitly imported from pub.
I believe doing so would lead to inconsistency and duplication of code across both packages.

I think not. I think the best Idea would be to have basic stuff inside flutter, like `RawScrollbar` etc. But everything that is different should be in a different package. Similar to how it is right now, but just that the package does not live inside flutter, but outside, which would have multiple advantages:
- If you don't use material or cupertino, but something like yaru or fluent, you are no longer annoyed by autocompletion that suggests you to use material or cupertino buttons. Sounds simple, but is a really bad user experience 
- The material / cupertino library can have easier breaking changes without needing tons of backwards compatibility by just increasing the major version number is something like material 3 or in the future perhaps material 4 is released. The developer does not need to update the package but can still upgrade flutter without needing to migrate the whole application to a major design system change
- Flutter becomes more like a better html / css kind of framework but without the html and css of course :D. Which is something I would like very much to happen. To have flutter just be the framework without making any assumptions as to what kind of design you want to implement. This would also make it more obvious to newcomers that flutter is not just to make material / cupertino apps.

I think in general flutter has a very weird approach with including a gui library within the framework. It is very unusual.

Competing modern frameworks don't do it either (I know this is a weak argument, but I wanted to mention it :D). Like react or react native or lit or vue.js etc. They all are just frameworks that give you a way to implement what ever design you want in html and css, which is similar to flutters basic widgets, but there are external web component libraries that you can use for example that will give you some sort of design system. And you can choose depending on what you want to do. I think this is how it should be.

I'm also in favor of removing specific ui libraries (material, cupertino) out of the "core" framework.
- Its better for maintenance if material & cupertino are not tied to the core framework as pointed out above (backwards compatibility, breaking changes, update cycle). See the discussion #91605
- It gives tribute to the variety of other component libraries ([flame-engine](https://github.com/flame-engine/flame), [fluent](https://pub.dev/packages/fluent), [macos_ui](https://pub.dev/packages/macos_ui), [yaru](https://pub.dev/packages/yaru),  [macos_ui](https://pub.dev/packages/macos_ui)) out there. Thats actually a very good development that highlights the power of the core flutter framework. Its not uncommon to include an template/starter system or just a list of options of ui libraries which could be downloaded via pub. Basically the same approach as with state management solutions (core widgets/classes + variety of community libraries) See the discussion #97496

*I don't see a "fragmented community" with this proposal just more choice and flexibility. If the material library should be the "de facto standard" for all platforms & use cases than its better to improve it and not bound it to the core framework. Also i'm not sure why cupertino is part of a cross-platform framework. Why should anyone use it on the web or desktop?!

copy paste from [duplicate](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/109145#issue-1331708337)

> (maybe[ this one](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/91605)?), but I couldn't find a "start to end" discussion about it

The problem with unbundling the `material` library is that `flutter create` has to create a template that uses a widget library of some sort, to help with the first hour experience (it's a lot more fun if you can just build an app without having to first figure out where to get buttons and so on). Most (all?) big platforms / UI frameworks / SDKs (e.g. the web, Windows, Android) do come with a baked-in set of widgets (actually Flutter is pretty unique in making its default set entirely optional and entirely defined in terms of lower-level primitives that you can just use directly; usually the default widgets are much more tightly integrated into the offering).

We could depend on a split-out `material` instead of bundling it with the SDK, but that would not really make any difference; you'd still need to pin the exact version for quality-control purposes, just like we do with vector_math. The effect would just be to slow development and make it harder for people to test their changes.

`material` depends on `cupertino` which is why we bundle `cupertino` as well.

Responding to some comments above:

From @nikitadol:

> * Long update cycle

You can use the `beta` channel if you want faster updates. It uses the same release process as `stable`, but is updated monthly instead of every quarter. (You could also just use `master`, but that has had less testing.)


> * To get the new version - you need to update the version of Flutter

As discussed above, the package and the SDK would be pinned in either case.


> * Random imports

Not sure what this means.


> * Components for other platforms are separate from Flutter ([fluent](https://pub.dev/packages/fluent), [macos_ui](https://pub.dev/packages/macos_ui), [yaru](https://pub.dev/packages/yaru), ...)

Only one widget set needs to ship with Flutter. We could talk about changing to another built-in set, e.g. using `yaru` as the out-of-the-box widget set, but I think in practice currently the material widgets are the most logical choice (since unlike most widget sets, they are designed to be cross-platform, like Flutter).

@LasseRosenow:

> * If you don't use material or cupertino, but something like yaru or fluent, you are no longer annoyed by autocompletion that suggests you to use material or cupertino buttons. Sounds simple, but is a really bad user experience

If autocomplete is annoying, please file a bug specifically on that. We should definitely not be surfacing things in autocomplete that you don't want.

> * The material / cupertino library can have easier breaking changes without needing tons of backwards compatibility by just increasing the major version number is something like material 3 or in the future perhaps material 4 is released. The developer does not need to update the package but can still upgrade flutter without needing to migrate the whole application to a major design system change

Unfortunately since we need to pin the material and flutter versions, this advantage is not available to us.


> * Flutter becomes more like a better html / css kind of framework but without the html and css of course :D. Which is something I would like very much to happen. To have flutter just be the framework without making any assumptions as to what kind of design you want to implement. This would also make it more obvious to newcomers that flutter is not just to make material / cupertino apps.

I agree that we should highlight other design languages. I'm not sure that this is more important than giving people a very fast out-of-box experience, though. Requiring that people think about what widget set they want when they're just experimenting with the framework is, IMHO, not conducive to a good experience.


@iapicca:

> having 2 separate packages would solve that

Having two packages would double the maintenance burden, which would just mean Material 2 no longer gets bug fixes.

> `uses-material-design`

I believe we could get rid of this now, we'd just need to move to handling the Material icons font like we do the Cupertino icons font. Please feel free to file a bug on this if it's important to you.

> * would make sense if [material.io](https://pub.dev/publishers/material.io) maintained it
>   (possibly and eventually, but definitely not happening if it's just bundled in the sdk)

Actually the material.io team is already involved in `package:material`'s creation. It would not substantially change who was developing it if it moved out of the SDK. (Actually if it moved out of the Flutter project's purview into a less open-source-friendly project, it would probably mean that fewer people could contribute, which seems net worse.)

> * a strength of `flutter` is the ease of implementation of "branding", but sometimes if hindered by over reliance on material widgets and it's limited extensibility of it, un-bundling it would/may "force" the package to be more interoperable with flutter's core widgets and their extensions

In practice you can just not import package:material. We enforce a layering principle in the SDK to make sure nothing lower-level depends on the `material` library.

Responding also to some comments in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/110195 from @alestiago:

> As user of Flutter I don't want to be able to import `material` or `cupertino`. I want to enforce developers to use their own design system package. This is particularly common in big organisations where the design system is strict. Moving this out of "flutter" will avoid organisations developing their own scripts or workaround to prohibit importing material or cupertino into the main application.

As far as I can tell there's no mechanism to prevent people from depending on a `pub` package any more than there is preventing them to depend on `package:material`. If you want to apply a style guide to disallow some package, it's relatively straight-forward to write a script to scan the code and flag uses of the banned package.

> As a Flutter developer I want to be able to update Flutter without updating material or cupertino. As Flutter has evolved, there are sometimes deprecations from material and in some applications, I don't want to update material (yet) but do want to update the framework itself.

As discussed above, even if we moved it into a separate package, it would still need to be pinned so that we could guarantee that the template app worked. So this would not solve that problem.

> The framework can be kept small by moving `material` and `cupertino` as their own independent packages.

We already do this by enforcing layering such that nothing outside of `material` depends on `material` (and nothing outside of `cupertino` and `material` depends on `cupertino`). It would not make a meaningful difference to the layering if it was in a separate package.


FWIW, I think there is a world where eventually we have `flutter create` build from templates found in pub packages, and in that world we could consider having a `material` package that has a template and then the testing burden shifts sufficiently that we could think about not pinning the framework to the material library (or vice versa), by having `flutter create` automatically fetch the `material` package. It would need to be done very carefully. It would be interesting, though, since it would let us propose other such packages in the installation instructions (e.g. "flutter create -p macos" to get a macos-based template). (cc @christopherfujino)

> FWIW, I think there is a world where eventually we have flutter create build from templates found in pub packages, and in that world we could consider having a material package that has a template and then the testing burden shifts sufficiently that we could think about not pinning the framework to the material library (or vice versa), by having flutter create automatically fetch the material package.

@Hixie this is exactly why I've been working on [`package:mason`](https://youtu.be/qjA0JFiPMnQ) 💙

`package:mason` allows developers to create, publish, and generate code from reusable templates called bricks via [`package:mason_cli`](https://pub.dev/packages/mason_cli).

Bricks can be hosted locally, via git, or registry (https://brickhub.dev). There are hundreds of examples currently available on https://brickhub.dev ranging from a [license file](https://brickhub.dev/bricks/licenser) to a [fully functional flutter application](https://brickhub.dev/bricks/very_good_core).

Something to consider if you feel mason aligns with the vision you have in mind.
Always happy to help, thanks!

> Requiring that people think about what widget set they want when they're just experimenting with the framework is, IMHO, not conducive to a good experience.

I think this really depends on what flutter tries to be. In my mind it could be a alternative to frameworks like `react`, `vue.js` etc. These frameworks would also not come with a build in widget library, because their job is to be only the framework / tool to build good apps. The widget library would then be the developers choice. Of course the framework could always pin out famous well maintained widget libraries to give the developer some advice, so they have a good experience.
=> That being said I REALLY LIKE the last part of your message:

> FWIW, I think there is a world where eventually we have flutter create build from templates found in pub packages, and in that world we could consider having a material package that has a template and then the testing burden shifts sufficiently that we could think about not pinning the framework to the material library (or vice versa), by having flutter create automatically fetch the material package. It would need to be done very carefully. It would be interesting, though, since it would let us propose other such packages in the installation instructions (e.g. "flutter create -p macos" to get a macos-based template). (cc @christopherfujino)

I think this is the way to go. It would really be amazing and it would fix all the issues raised in this thread. Completely untie flutter with the platform specific ui libraries like cupertino and material, but provide a easy setup experience that is even better than today and gives the opportunity to select one of many recommended ui libraries :) And having the ability to choose a library for a specific platform would be great like `windows: fluent_ui` or `android: material`, `macos: macos_ui` etc. and maybe even recommending to use `flutter_platform_widgets` if the developer chooses to develop `platform independent` gui. :)
Referencing https://github.com/flame-engine/flame/issues/688, since if Material and Cupertino are ever moved outside the engine having LTS support might be beneficial.

_Originally posted in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/91605#issuecomment-1154573877_

----

Not to underestimate the amount of work this would take, I want to discuss separating Material and Cupertino widgets from the main Flutter repo (and included by default in the `pubspec.yaml` from `flutter create`) for several reasons: 
- It enables Flutter and Material to have different views on breaking changes. For example, Flutter seems to be very against breaking changes, favoring a time-based deprecation scheme instead of semantic versioning. Dart also uses its own language versioning process. A separate package would allow the widgets to stay in sync with the Material spec, independent of Flutter's policies (not that I disagree with those policies).
- On the same note, if a dev prefers, they can mix and match versions of Flutter and Material (to an extent, probably). For example, if a dev was on Flutter 2, they could easily make the purely technical switch to Flutter 3 and gain all the performance improvements without committing to the stylistic changes of Material 3.
- This platform-agnostic separation would make using other platforms feel less hacky. It's no doubt that Flutter intends for Material to be the "default", and for documentation purposes, it still could be. Have all the codelabs follow Material, sure. But today, adding support for other platforms is not straightforward. 
  - Some widgets have a `.adaptive` constructor, like [`Switch.adaptive`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/Switch/Switch.adaptive.html). It is unclear which platforms these constructors support since they are included in the `material` library. Also, these widgets can't take advantage of other supported platforms like `fluent_ui` since that's a separate package. 
  - Other widgets have platform adaptive behavior, like `Scrollable`. When over-scrolled, it sends [`GlowingOverscrollIndicator`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/GlowingOverscrollIndicator-class.html) or [`BouncingScrollPhysics`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/StretchingOverscrollIndicator-class.html) notifications, depending on the platform. It can even send [`StretchingOverscrollIndicator`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/StretchingOverscrollIndicator-class.html) notifications like Material 3. Again, it's unclear which behavior will be chosen. Again, it is also impossible for these widgets to function correctly on platforms with their own package, like `fluent_ui`. 
  - Other widgets are split between `material`, `cupertino`, `fluent_ui`, and other libraries and must be chosen manually. Given the range of behaviors listed above, it's unclear when to rely on the default Material widget or find an alternative in these massive libraries. As mentioned, some even depend on others, making them not truly independent. Also, the alternative widgets don't feel official since they're prefixed by platform. Between `CupertinoTheme` and `Theme`, it feels like `Theme` is the "correct" version to use. 
- This will make it easier for developers to introduce their own globally themed widgets. Separately from using `ThemeData`, there are cases where one wants every or most Material widget to be replaced with their own. A modular framework allows devs to create their own widgets, hardly interacting with the default Material library. One example would be when porting Flutter to a new platform. Another example is a wiki site for a game that wants to have buttons, text, and displays themed like the game's UI. For an example, see [this page](https://www.factorio.com/blog/) from Factorio's website. Imagine importing `package:factorio` instead of `material` to create that site. 

TL;DR: Separating platform widgets into their own package is useful for breaking changes, but it primarily declares an intent to support a given platform's conventions. While this doesn't have to replace the "out-of-the-box" support for a reasonable default like Material, it does allow each platform to take its own spin on Flutter. 

----

Then there's the problem of easily supporting different platforms' conventions by default, as Flutter currently does. I'm not sure the _best_ way to do this, but from my last bullet, one can make an umbrella package that automatically chooses the right widget for the user's platform, and users would import that. So imagine a `mobile_ui` package with its own `AlertDialog` that chooses between a Material dialog on Android or a `CupertinoDialog` on iOS.

----

> As discussed above, even if we moved it into a separate package, it would still need to be pinned so that we could guarantee that the template app worked. So this would not solve that problem.

@Hixie can you elaborate on this a bit more -- if Flutter moves to Flutter 3, what stops it from supporting Material 2 code? That is, even if `package:material` uploads a `v3.0`, why can't we have: 
```YAML
dependencies:
  flutter:
    sdk: flutter  # Flutter 3 is installed
  material: ^2.0
```

The `flutter` _tool_ could generate a `pubspec.yaml` that includes `material: ^<latest>` for `flutter create`, just as it currently does with `lints` and `flutter_lints`.

> The problem with unbundling the `material` library is that `flutter create` has to create a template that uses a widget library of some sort [...] We could depend on a split-out `material` instead of bundling it with the SDK, but that would not really make any difference; you'd still need to pin the exact version for quality-control purposes, just like we do with vector_math.

Would the SDK itself actually need to pin a version? If the pin were just in the template, then it becomes (like all of the other template files) user-editable. So we would have a controlled, known-good out-of-the-box experience, but developers could then adjust the version as they saw fit (unlike `vector_math`).

Its difficult to respond to every point made here, I think I generally agree with @Hixie 's statements above.

While I understand the concerns about velocity, release frequency, breaking changes - I can't possible agree that the specific solution of splitting up packages solves these. There are serious trade-offs to be made here, and the path that we have chosen has been intentional to ensure that we concentrate our resources on shipping a coherent experience instead of dividing up into dozens of small pieces that users must discover and assemble themselves. Beyond that, there are also plenty of "implementation details" in both our benchmarking, performance testing, unit testing, integration testing, infrastructure, and devicelab that rely on our particular infrastructure set up. In my experience, the more layers a repository is divded up into, the more complicated the infrastructure becomes and the harder it can be to deal with common development workflows like reverts and cherry-picks.

> Would the SDK itself actually need to pin a version?

The SDK would need to have a version that is used throughout tests and so forth. It's true that in some configurations this would not necessarily prevent people from using a different version in their app (though that combination would likely be untested and thus likely to break a lot in practice).

> * It enables Flutter and Material to have different views on breaking changes. For example, Flutter seems to be very against breaking changes

Flutter's actually very aggressive with breaking changes in the SDK, more so than we are in packages. Splitting the material library into a package would probably slow down the rate of breaking changes rather than speed it up (because we have a policy of supporting the last stable + the main branch for packages, whereas the SDK only needs to support itself).

> * On the same note, if a dev prefers, they can mix and match versions of Flutter and Material (to an extent, probably). For example, if a dev was on Flutter 2, they could easily make the purely technical switch to Flutter 3 and gain all the performance improvements without committing to the stylistic changes of Material 3.

Only if Flutter 3 didn't break something that Material 2 depends on.


>   * Some widgets have a `.adaptive` constructor, like [`Switch.adaptive`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/Switch/Switch.adaptive.html). It is unclear which platforms these constructors support since they are included in the `material` library. Also, these widgets can't take advantage of other supported platforms like `fluent_ui` since that's a separate package.

They are intended to support what the Material spec says to support, which in practice is basically just iOS as I understand it.

(Making it a separate package wouldn't affect this and would not make it possible for material to depend on other packages because we would want the "default" package to have no extra dependencies.)


>   * Other widgets have platform adaptive behavior, like `Scrollable`. When over-scrolled, it sends [`GlowingOverscrollIndicator`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/GlowingOverscrollIndicator-class.html) or [`BouncingScrollPhysics`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/StretchingOverscrollIndicator-class.html) notifications, depending on the platform. It can even send [`StretchingOverscrollIndicator`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/StretchingOverscrollIndicator-class.html) notifications like Material 3. Again, it's unclear which behavior will be chosen. Again, it is also impossible for these widgets to function correctly on platforms with their own package, like `fluent_ui`.

This is unaffected by splitting material out. Please file bugs if these are issues you are running into.


>   * Other widgets are split between `material`, `cupertino`, `fluent_ui`, and other libraries and must be chosen manually. Given the range of behaviors listed above, it's unclear when to rely on the default Material widget or find an alternative in these massive libraries. As mentioned, some even depend on others, making them not truly independent. Also, the alternative widgets don't feel official since they're prefixed by platform. Between `CupertinoTheme` and `Theme`, it feels like `Theme` is the "correct" version to use.

This would be unaffected by splitting material out as far as I can tell.


> * This will make it easier for developers to introduce their own globally themed widgets. Separately from using `ThemeData`, there are cases where one wants every or most Material widget to be replaced with their own. A modular framework allows devs to create their own widgets, hardly interacting with the default Material library. One example would be when porting Flutter to a new platform. Another example is a wiki site for a game that wants to have buttons, text, and displays themed like the game's UI. For an example, see [this page](https://www.factorio.com/blog/) from Factorio's website. Imagine importing `package:factorio` instead of `material` to create that site.

I agree with your goals here but that seems entirely possible today and unrelated to splitting out material.


> @Hixie can you elaborate on this a bit more -- if Flutter moves to Flutter 3, what stops it from supporting Material 2 code? That is, even if `package:material` uploads a `v3.0`, why can't we have:
> 
> ```yaml
> dependencies:
>   flutter:
>     sdk: flutter  # Flutter 3 is installed
>   material: ^2.0
> ```
> 
> The `flutter` _tool_ could generate a `pubspec.yaml` that includes `material: ^<latest>` for `flutter create`, just as it currently does with `lints` and `flutter_lints`.

In principle this would be possible. In practice it's extremely likely that the package would still be tightly coupled with the core framework since new features usually depend on new features in the framework, and changes to the core framework usually require changes to the higher layers.

> > * On the same note, if a dev prefers, they can mix and match versions of Flutter and Material (to an extent, probably). For example, if a dev was on Flutter 2, they could easily make the purely technical switch to Flutter 3 and gain all the performance improvements without committing to the stylistic changes of Material 3.
> 
> Only if Flutter 3 didn't break something that Material 2 depends on.

This is the part I didn't give enough attention to in my argument, but ultimately doesn't change the status quo -- if Flutter 3 does breaks something important that Material 2 depends on, users can still use Flutter 2 while transitioning to Material 3, and then upgrade to Flutter 3 when that's done. This way, they can focus on one kind of breaking change at a time. Or they can stay on Flutter 2, either way, it's not much different than right now. 

Well, the difference would be `material: ^3.0.0` in the Pubspec rather than `useMaterial3` in `ThemeData`.

> > * Some widgets have a `.adaptive` constructor, like [`Switch.adaptive`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/Switch/Switch.adaptive.html). It is unclear which platforms these constructors support since they are included in the `material` library. Also, these widgets can't take advantage of other supported platforms like `fluent_ui` since that's a separate package.
> 
> They are intended to support what the Material spec says to support, which in practice is basically just iOS as I understand it.

The Material spec for the [M3 switch](https://m3.material.io/components/switch) doesn't say anything about Cupertino, that's the point of this issue. The link to the Flutter docs from that page goes to [`ThemeData.useMaterial3`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/ThemeData/useMaterial3.html), which doesn't discuss switches at all. In the "Controls" section of [Material's cross-platform guidelines](https://m2.material.io/design/platform-guidance/cross-platform-adaptation.html#cross-platform-guidelines), it does say to use a native switch when on iOS, but that's only so helpful when Flutter supports MacOS, Windows, and Linux. In other words, it's unspecified.

The issue goes beyond `Switch`, though. At least `Switch` _has_ a `.adaptive` constructor. Plenty of widgets don't, and just pick and choose whether they'll be platform-adaptive or not without making it clear, hence my comment here:

> * Other widgets have platform adaptive behavior, like `Scrollable`. When over-scrolled, it sends [`GlowingOverscrollIndicator`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/GlowingOverscrollIndicator-class.html) or [`BouncingScrollPhysics`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/StretchingOverscrollIndicator-class.html) notifications, depending on the platform. It can even send [`StretchingOverscrollIndicator`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/StretchingOverscrollIndicator-class.html) notifications like Material 3. Again, it's unclear which behavior will be chosen. Again, it is also impossible for these widgets to function correctly on platforms with their own package, like `fluent_ui`.

Next point:

> > * Other widgets are split between `material`, `cupertino`, `fluent_ui`, and other libraries and must be chosen manually. Given the range of behaviors listed above, it's unclear when to rely on the default Material widget or find an alternative in these massive libraries. As mentioned, some even depend on others, making them not truly independent. Also, the alternative widgets don't feel official since they're prefixed by platform. Between `CupertinoTheme` and `Theme`, it feels like `Theme` is the "correct" version to use.
> 
> This would be unaffected by splitting material out as far as I can tell.

It would be, because users would acknowledge that they're importing `material`, and could just as well choose to import `cupertino` instead. By uncoupling "Material" from "Flutter", users can choose to fully use Cupertino or Fluent without lingering references to Material. As I said, `Theme` is Material but Cupertino has `CupertinoTheme`. Imagine if they were both `Theme` but one was `package:material` and the other was `package:cupertino`. 



> > * This will make it easier for developers to introduce their own globally themed widgets. Separately from using `ThemeData`, there are cases where one wants every or most Material widget to be replaced with their own. A modular framework allows devs to create their own widgets, hardly interacting with the default Material library. One example would be when porting Flutter to a new platform. Another example is a wiki site for a game that wants to have buttons, text, and displays themed like the game's UI. For an example, see [this page](https://www.factorio.com/blog/) from Factorio's website. Imagine importing `package:factorio` instead of `material` to create that site.
> 
> I agree with your goals here but that seems entirely possible today and unrelated to splitting out material.

Not really? Sure you can have `FluentText` and `FluentButton`, but it makes it _so_ much harder and more awkward to use. It's similar to my comments above about Cupertino. It feels like Flutter is Material-first, then "whatever we can fit about Cupertino later". I'm all for supporting Material _by default_ and having tutorials import `package:material`, but it should at least be possible to get a pleasant dev experience using anything else.

For the record, I'm only advocating for this on a best-effort basis. Keeping Material 2 updated alongside the latest Flutter for a long time when Material 3 is already out? That's a bit unecessary. While I'd appreciate some official workflow to switch from Material to Fluent, it also makes complete sense for most tutorials to exclusively focus on Material. And if Material is Flutter's first choice, it'd also make sense that only Material be given priority for updates and bug fixes.

But even with all that, it would still help the community if they could build an ecosystem that was capable of replacing Material, even if the Flutter team never officially takes that on.

> > > if a dev was on Flutter 2, they could easily make the purely technical switch to Flutter 3 and gain all the performance improvements without committing to the stylistic changes of Material 3.
> > 
> > Only if Flutter 3 didn't break something that Material 2 depends on.
> 
> This is the part I didn't give enough attention to in my argument, but ultimately doesn't change the status quo -- if Flutter 3 does breaks something important that Material 2 depends on, users can still use Flutter 2 while transitioning to Material 3

Only if Material 3 didn't depend on anything new in Flutter 3.

Both of these conditions (new Flutter versions not breaking old Material versions, and new Material versions not depending on new Flutter versions) are things beyond just splitting out the package that would dramatically slow the development of Material. You can see these constraints in play a bit with our current packages actually — it can take months for changes to land in some of our plugins because of our policy of needing to support both the last stable and the current master branch. What we would need to do to support the scenario you describe is even more constrained — support the last _two_ stable releases _and_ commit to not breaking the last major revision of the package.

The `material` library so closely depends on the `widgets` library because the work on `material` drives our work on core library features. This is good. You don't want a team to be working in a vacuum, inventing core APIs without proving them with a real world widget set. As our [style guide / design philosophy wiki page](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Style-guide-for-Flutter-repo#solve-real-problems-by-literally-solving-a-real-problem) says, "solve real problems by literally solving a real problem". We happen to have picked Material because the people who started Flutter worked closely with the people who made Material and none of the other widget sets at the time had a real specification, but what matters is just that there be _a_ widget set that drives the core development, whatever it is.

Splitting out the widget set into its own repo while keeping it as the driving force for development is definitely something that _could_ be done, but it would be a _significant_ limiting factor on the velocity of the team. It's not at all clear to me that the trade-off is an obvious win.


> > > * Some widgets have a `.adaptive` constructor, like [`Switch.adaptive`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/Switch/Switch.adaptive.html). It is unclear which platforms these constructors support since they are included in the `material` library. Also, these widgets can't take advantage of other supported platforms like `fluent_ui` since that's a separate package.
> > 
> > They are intended to support what the Material spec says to support, which in practice is basically just iOS as I understand it.
> 
> The Material spec for the [M3 switch](https://m3.material.io/components/switch) doesn't say anything about Cupertino, that's the point of this issue.

Yeah, I'm not sure what the intent of the current spec is. Previous versions specifically called out iOS for a few specific widgets. It's possible that for Material 3 we should actually remove all the adaptive constructors. That's probably a topic for another issue.


> * Other widgets have platform adaptive behavior, like `Scrollable`. [...] it's unclear which behavior will be chosen.

These behaviours are defined in the `widgets` library. To the extent that things are unclear, please file bugs for us to improve the documentation.


> Again, it is also impossible for these widgets to function correctly on platforms with their own package, like `fluent_ui`.

Per our current design, scrolling behaviour should be in `widgets`. If there's a platform supported by `TargetPlatform` that doesn't have corresponding logic in `widgets`, please file a bug.

I don't think moving the `material` library to a package would lead to us implementing scrolling by depending on `fluent_ui`. (Indeed it seems unlikely that we could since `fluent_ui` itself depends on `material` currently.)


> It would be, because users would acknowledge that they're importing `material`, and could just as well choose to import `cupertino` instead.

That's true today too as far as I can tell.


> By uncoupling "Material" from "Flutter", users can choose to fully use Cupertino or Fluent without lingering references to Material.

That's also true today (well, except that Fluent is defined in terms of Material). I don't see why that would change as a result of moving Material to a package.


> As I said, `Theme` is Material but Cupertino has `CupertinoTheme`. Imagine if they were both `Theme` but one was `package:material` and the other was `package:cupertino`.

We could do that today too, without moving these libraries into separate packages. We intentionally named everything in the Cupertino package with the Cupertino prefix. This is similar to how many libraries prefix a lot of their classes with a common name, e.g. the [Arna](https://pub.dev/documentation/arna/latest/) package prefixes everything with `Arna`.


> Sure you can have `FluentText` and `FluentButton`, but it makes it _so_ much harder and more awkward to use.

I don't understand why you can't just have `Text` and `Button` while using Fluent. I mean, you literally can in fact; `Text` comes from the `widgets` library and `Button` is what `fluent_ui` calls its buttons.


> It's similar to my comments above about Cupertino. It feels like Flutter is Material-first, then "whatever we can fit about Cupertino later".

That's an accurate description of our past investments, unfortunately. (We've recently increased our investment in iOS in general and Cupertino specifically quite a bit, so I hope we'll start seeing lots of improvements to Cupertino.)

But that's entirely independent of whether things are in the main flutter repo or the package repo. Indeed historically I would say we've often neglected our packages more than the main repo!


> I'm all for supporting Material _by default_ and having tutorials import `package:material`, but it should at least be possible to get a pleasant dev experience using anything else.

If the experience is unpleasant using other widget sets, please file bugs. I don't see any reason why this should be affected by where `material` is.


> But even with all that, it would still help the community if they could build an ecosystem that was capable of replacing Material, even if the Flutter team never officially takes that on.

I am very happy to encourage this, and indeed some of my work recently has been at looking at what we can do to encourage more widget sets to bloom, but that's entirely orthogonal to the question of which repository the code for the material library lives in.

### Recap of the whole case: Flutters Cupertino & Material packages are hard to change frequently, especially for minimal changes that could make a huge difference for the developers and users, so the solution is that they should be decoupled from the Flutter core packages and be a separate [pub](https://pub.dev/) package. Flutter has a set of core packages built into it, including the UI packages [Material](https://docs.flutter.dev/ui/widgets/material) and [Cupertino](https://docs.flutter.dev/ui/widgets/cupertino) which are the corresponding UI designs for iOS and Android, but when it comes to using platform native UI on desktop there are packages but not tightly coupled to the framework for Windows there is the [flutent_ui](https://pub.dev/packages/fluent_ui) package and for macOS there is the [macos_ui](https://pub.dev/packages/macos_ui). ### Why am I mentioning the macOS and Windows UI packages? if you notice, when checking out these packages on pub.dev, the first thing you will immediately notice is how recent the package is updated, even better check the versions tab and look at how frequently they get updated, why is it important on how frequent these packages are updated? To me it suggests that a lot of arguments against decoupling `material` and `cupertino` from Flutter are mostly hypothetical. I think it's important to untangle a few things.

**What should be moved to the core framework?**
What Widgets/functionality is there now in material that is critical enough that should be moved to the core framework or, like others pointed out, what are the gaps? I think of things like ReorderableListView, DataTable, ... 
A look at UI libraries that mostly don't use Material or Cupertino (which are rare) like [Forui](https://forui.dev) could give us a real hint. Would be nice if we could manage to come up with the most critical ones somewhere.

**A set of basic widgets**
What we should not wait for in my opinion is something like the [blankcanvas](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1rS_RO2DQ_d4_roc3taAB6vXFjv7-9hJP7pyZ9NhPOdA/edit?resourcekey=0-VBzTPoqLwsruo0j9dokuOg&tab=t.0#heading=h.34a91yqebirw) proposal or in general a basic set of widgets that somehow pops up that would be implemented by most UI frameworks to reduce boilerplate and improve the interoperability between frameworks. While that sounds nice, in theory it delays everything infinitely. It would take endless discussions about what should be part of that and how the specifics of such a library should look like. I have yet to see that some unstyled frameworks ever managed to be a real backbone of all kinds of UI libraries.
If we would have just a few solid UI libraries that are customizable enough and would not depend on Material/Cupertino, most devs would be happy enough. 

**What should we do with Material/Cupertino?**
Nothing?! Like some pointed out, cupertino has no dependencies on material and material is in all kinds of ways dependent on cupertino. 
In my opinion, it should not really be our business as long as those packages are moved out of the core framework. Release cupertino as its own package, release material as its own package with a dependency on cupertino, or release both together as one package and name it "materialino". We shouldn't really care. Google wired them the way they are and has ongoing specs for them that are not part of an open discussion.

> I would love to hear remarks from people working on bigger pieces of widgets like [@Piinks](https://github.com/Piinks)...

Sure, I will share my 2 cents for what it is worth. 😜 

We've been focused on a couple of projects right now, but one of the bigger efforts is the [Cupertino refresh project](https://github.com/orgs/flutter/projects/155/views/6?groupedBy%5BcolumnId%5D=123151514), bringing that package back up to par with its Material counterpart, and creating UIs that feel at home on iOS. 

As part of that work, we have been identifying and relocating core components _out of Material_ and _into the Widgets_ layer where appropriate. We've been able to do this carefully and without introducing breaks because the Material & Cupertino libraries export the widgets library. 🚀 
Some good examples of this are [ToggleableStateMixin](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/148272), [RawMenuAnchor](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/158255), and wip [ExpansibleStateMixin](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/162702).

Having a more robust widgets layer for core components not only allows Cupertino and Material to share common function and implementation, which is awesome for those libraries to be consistent, share code where they can and have all those benefits - but as we are _really_ hoping to achieve - makes it easier as well for other design libraries to be implemented. We've been trying to really test ourselves here in updating Cupertino, which cannot depend on Material, to identify any gaps in the widgets layer for building common UI components and fix them.

> IMO, one way around this would be to create a third package called adaptative, which would contain all the widgets that adapt according to the platform. This way, we could even remove the meaningless .adaptative constructors from the material widgets.

👀 🤭 ^ This is actually something that has come up. I want to be clear that this idea has not gotten to the point of a proposal that is ready for feedback, but I am encouraged to see it mentioned here - thanks for sharing! Having a better adaptive story is also something we are working towards. One of the motivations behind the Cupertino refresh project is to have better consistency between Cupertino and Material so that that adaptive experience can be that much better. We have not been adding more .adaptive constructors in Material while we're considering the user journey for platform adaptive UIs, and whether or not the adaptive experience should even come from within the Material library - versus another package like `adaptive`. IMO, I really like the idea of having a dedicated library for platform adaptive widgets, and would prefer it designed in such a way that does not only support Material and Cupertino, but allows other design languages to be plugged in too, like [fluent_ui](https://pub.dev/packages/fluent_ui) or [macos_ui](https://pub.dev/packages/macos_ui).  I expect we will start to dig into this work as we get closer to finishing our work in Cupertino.


To the original question, why don't we move these libraries out of the core framework? Unfortunately, I can't really speak to any hard or fast rules around this. This issue is currently open for consideration. With all the work I laid out above that the team is working towards, I don't think we will be working on moving those packages out of the core framework in the near term. I can share that doing something like that would be a lot of work for the team, with not much material change - pun not intended 😉. I did have a proposal last year to [move the M2 implementation out of the framework](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1REsg982YG2MlspsMOiNLpu8OIWkyehRRQUXHMU3tAAM/edit?usp=sharing), but it was found to just not be feasible to extract it without introducing regressions and significant breaking changes, while also needing to stand up new infrastructure and CI/CD to continue to support it elsewhere.

Thanks for the continued feedback and brainstorming, it's very helpful. 😊
